message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Point didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the lighthouse keeper, the silence was a companion he had learned to respect. For forty years, he had climbed the three hundred steps of the spiral stair, polished the Fresnel lens, and watched the Atlantic chew at the rocks below. He was a man of routine, of salt-cured habits, and of solitude so deep it had become part of his bones.

The bottle arrived on a Tuesday, wedged into a crevice of the basalt cliffs like a secret the ocean was finally ready to spit out. It was old, green glass, sealed with wax that had cracked like dried mud. Elias picked it up, expecting the usual: a love letter from a sailor long dead, a plea for help from a ship that had already sunk, or perhaps just sand.

But when he uncorked it, the paper inside was crisp. The ink was fresh.

*“Elias,”* it read. *“Don’t turn off the light tonight.”*

He laughed, a dry, rattling sound in the empty tower. A prank. It had to be. But who would know his name? Who would have access to the supply boat that docked only once a month? He looked at the handwriting. It was jagged, hurried, but unmistakably familiar. It was his own handwriting.

Elias frowned. He checked the date scrawled in the corner of the note: *October 14, 2024.*

Today was October 14, 2024.

A cold prickle danced down his spine. He looked out the window. The fog was thickening, turning the world into a gray void. The lighthouse beam swept across the water, a golden eye blinking in the dark. Protocol dictated that during severe
